guidelineReferences.name: AAD Contact Dermatitis Guidelines
keyPoints: Identify and eliminate the offending agent
Patch testing for chronic or recurrent dermatitis
Topical corticosteroids are mainstay of treatment
High-potency steroids for limited areas, not face/folds
Systemic steroids for severe or widespread involvement
Emollients and barrier creams for prevention

female-35-contact.name: Stephanie Lopez
female-35-contact.chiefComplaint: Itchy red rash on my hands for about 2 weeks
female-35-contact.variantDescription: Allergic contact dermatitis from occupational exposure

missedConsequences.Contact Dermatitis: Untreated contact dermatitis becomes chronic, leading to lichenification and potentially occupational disability.
missedConsequences.Cellulitis: Missed secondary bacterial infection spreads and becomes systemic.
missedConsequences.Scabies: Missed infestation leads to ongoing transmission to close contacts.

commonPitfalls: Not taking detailed exposure history including occupational and home products
Missing subtle signs of secondary infection
Prescribing topical steroids without addressing the cause
Not considering scabies in itchy rash with appropriate exposure
Failing to discuss allergen avoidance as primary treatment

mcqs.question: Which distribution pattern is MOST suggestive of allergic contact dermatitis?

options.explanation: Flexural involvement is more typical of atopic dermatitis.
Contact dermatitis often has geometric patterns reflecting where the allergen contacted skin, with sharp demarcation.
Dermatomal distribution suggests herpes zoster.
Web space involvement is classic for scabies.

mcqs.question: The MOST effective long-term management of allergic contact dermatitis is:

options.explanation: Long-term topical steroids cause skin atrophy and are not appropriate for chronic management.

options.text: Identification and avoidance of the allergen
options.explanation: Allergen avoidance is the only way to achieve lasting resolution. Steroids treat symptoms but not the cause.
Antihistamines help itching but don't address the underlying cause.
Immunotherapy is not standard treatment for contact dermatitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pattern follows exposure areas (hands), timeline correlates with new product use, vesicles and itch present.

allDiagnoses.name: Irritant Contact Dermatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Similar presentation. Caused by chemical irritation rather than true allergy. Often coexists with allergic type.

allDiagnoses.name: Atopic Dermatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect flexural involvement and chronic history from childhood. Patient has no atopic history.

allDiagnoses.name: Dyshidrotic Eczema
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would see vesicles on palms and sides of fingers. Often stress-related.

allDiagnoses.name: Scabies
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider if close contacts are itchy or if web spaces involved. Look for burrows.

allDiagnoses.name: Tinea Manuum
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have central clearing and scaly border. Usually unilateral.

allDiagnoses.name: Psoriasis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect silvery scales and involvement of other classic sites (scalp, elbows, knees).

female-35-contact.oldcartsNarrative: "This rash started about 2 weeks ago on my hands. It's incredibly itchy - I can't stop scratching. It's red, kind of bumpy, and there are some tiny blisters. Mostly on the backs of my hands and between my fingers. It's about a 6 out of 10 for the itch. I've been using cortisone cream from the pharmacy which helps a little but it keeps coming back. I work as a hairdresser and I'm on my feet washing and styling hair all day. My hands are always in water and products."

historyQuestions.text: Have you started using any new products recently?
historyQuestions.answer: The salon switched to a new brand of shampoo about 3 weeks ago. And a new hair dye brand.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline correlates with new product exposure. Hair products are common contact allergens.

historyQuestions.text: Do you wear gloves at work?
historyQuestions.answer: Not usually for shampooing. I do wear them for coloring but not always for other stuff.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Lack of consistent protection exposes hands to irritants and allergens. Key intervention point.

historyQuestions.text: Is the rash only on your hands or anywhere else?
historyQuestions.answer: Mostly my hands. A little bit on my wrists where water drips down. Nowhere else really.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Localized pattern following exposure supports contact mechanism rather than systemic cause.

historyQuestions.text: Does anything make it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: It definitely flares up after busy days at work when I've washed lots of heads.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Work exposure as aggravating factor confirms occupational relationship.

historyQuestions.text: Any new products at home - soaps, lotions, detergents?
historyQuestions.answer: Not that I can think of. I use the same stuff I always have at home.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Helps narrow down occupational vs. home exposure.

historyQuestions.text: Is anyone else in your household itchy?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just me. My husband and kids are fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No household contacts affected argues against scabies.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have a history of eczema, asthma, or allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: I had some eczema as a kid but nothing in years. I do have seasonal allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Atopic background increases risk for contact sensitization.

historyQuestions.text: Any fever or feeling sick?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I feel fine. Just this maddening itch.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No systemic symptoms argues against secondary infection.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just birth control pills. And I've been using the OTC cortisone cream.
historyQuestions.reasoning: OCP noted; cortisone cream providing partial relief suggests steroid-responsive condition

historyQuestions.text: Any medication or other allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm allergic to nickel - can't wear cheap jewelry. And I'm allergic to cats.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nickel allergy shows predisposition to contact sensitization - may react to other allergens too

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm pretty healthy. Just the childhood eczema and seasonal allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Atopic background increases risk for contact dermatitis

historyQuestions.text: Any oozing, crusting, or spreading of the rash?
historyQuestions.answer: Some of the blisters have popped from scratching. A little oozing but no pus.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing for secondary bacterial infection from excoriation

historyQuestions.text: Do you use latex gloves at work?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, when I do wear gloves they're latex. Could that be a problem?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Latex is common allergen - may need to switch to nitrile gloves

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your work?
historyQuestions.answer: It's hard to focus when I'm itching constantly. I've been hiding my hands from clients because they look terrible.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing occupational and psychosocial impact

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_current_duties.answer: Hairdresser for 12 years. Shampooing, cutting, coloring, perms. Hands are wet most of the day.
occupation_current_duties.relevance: Hairdressing is high-risk occupation for contact dermatitis due to wet work and chemical exposures.

occupation_product_changes.answer: We switched to a new product line about 3 weeks ago. New shampoos, conditioners, and dyes.
occupation_product_changes.relevance: Timeline of new products correlates exactly with symptom onset - highly likely causative.

occupation_protection.answer: I wear gloves for coloring but not for shampooing or other stuff. They slow me down.
occupation_protection.relevance: Inconsistent glove use is major risk factor. Barrier protection is primary prevention.

occupation_frequency.answer: Full-time, 5 days a week. Busy salon - I might wash 20 heads a day.
occupation_frequency.relevance: High frequency of exposure increases risk and severity of contact dermatitis.

housing_home_exposures.answer: Nothing new at home. Same detergent, same hand soap. No gardening or hobbies with chemicals.
housing_home_exposures.relevance: Helps isolate occupational exposure as the cause.

social_stress.answer: Work has been stressful lately. We're short-staffed.
social_stress.relevance: Stress can exacerbate eczema but not primary cause here.

hobbies_exposures.answer: No crafts or hobbies with chemicals. I read, watch TV. Nothing messy.
hobbies_exposures.relevance: Rules out recreational sources of exposure.

social_hand_care.answer: I use hand lotion when I remember. My hands get so dry from all the washing.
social_hand_care.relevance: Dry skin from wet work impairs barrier function and increases sensitization risk.

physicalExam.finding: Vital signs normal. Afebrile.
physicalExam.reasoning: No fever argues against secondary infection.

physicalExam.finding: Erythematous, edematous plaques on dorsal hands bilaterally. Scattered vesicles at margins. Geometric borders corresponding to glove line. Some excoriations from scratching.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic contact pattern - geometric borders, vesicles, dorsal involvement. Excoriations show severity of itch.

physicalExam.finding: Web spaces erythematous but no burrows visualized. No involvement of sides of fingers.
physicalExam.reasoning: No burrows in web spaces argues against scabies.

physicalExam.finding: Palms relatively spared. Mild dryness and scaling but no vesicles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Sparing of palms argues against dyshidrotic eczema which typically involves palmar surface.

physicalExam.finding: Nails intact without pitting or dystrophy.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal nails argue against psoriasis and fungal infection.

physicalExam.text: Examine arms and rest of body
physicalExam.finding: Rash extends to wrists in splash pattern. Arms, trunk, legs clear. Flexural areas uninvolved.
physicalExam.reasoning: Splash pattern supports contact mechanism. No flexural involvement argues against atopic dermatitis.

physicalExam.text: Check epitrochlear and axillary lymph nodes
physicalExam.finding: No lymphadenopathy.
physicalExam.reasoning: No lymphadenopathy argues against infection.

labs.name: Labs generally not indicated for typical contact dermatitis
labs.result: Contact dermatitis is a clinical diagnosis based on history and examination pattern
labs.reasoning: Diagnosis is clinical. The pattern of distribution matching exposure is diagnostic.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.reasoning: Would only check if considering systemic cause or if eosinophilia might support allergic etiology. Does not change management.

labs.name: Total IgE level
labs.result: Not useful for contact dermatitis diagnosis
labs.reasoning: IgE is relevant for Type 1 hypersensitivity (urticaria, anaphylaxis). Contact dermatitis is Type IV (delayed) hypersensitivity - IgE is not involved.

labs.name: KOH prep
labs.result: Consider if fungal infection in differential
labs.reasoning: If considering tinea manuum (hand fungus), KOH can identify hyphae. Usually not needed when distribution clearly matches contact pattern.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.result: N/A - contact dermatitis is a clinical diagnosis
imaging.reasoning: No role for imaging in contact dermatitis. Diagnosis is purely clinical.

procedures.name: Patch testing (TRUE Test or custom panels)
procedures.result: Referral to dermatology for comprehensive patch testing to identify specific allergen. Results read at 48-96 hours.
procedures.reasoning: GOLD STANDARD for identifying contact allergens. Essential for occupational cases to document specific sensitizer. Standard panels test 35+ common allergens. Custom panels for occupational exposures (hair dye components, rubber accelerators, etc.).

procedures.name: Skin biopsy
procedures.result: Not typically needed for classic presentation
procedures.reasoning: Reserve for atypical presentations or to exclude other diagnoses (psoriasis, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma). Biopsy shows spongiotic dermatitis - not specific for contact vs other eczemas.

treatments.name: Allergen avoidance and barrier protection
treatments.result: Counseled extensively: Avoid suspected products. Wear nitrile gloves for ALL wet work. Cotton liners under gloves for comfort. Pat dry, don't rub.
treatments.reasoning: PRIMARY TREATMENT. Without removing the allergen, topical treatment will fail. Barrier protection prevents recurrence.

treatments.name: Topical corticosteroid (medium potency for hands)
treatments.result: Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment - apply twice daily to affected areas for 2 weeks. Ointment preferred over cream for barrier function.
treatments.reasoning: Hands require medium-high potency due to thick stratum corneum. Ointment better than cream (fewer additives, better barrier). Limit to 2-4 weeks on hands to avoid atrophy.

treatments.name: Barrier repair emollient
treatments.result: CeraVe, Vanicream, or Eucerin - apply liberally multiple times daily, especially after handwashing. Fragrance-free and dye-free.
treatments.reasoning: Barrier repair is crucial. Impaired barrier allows further allergen penetration. Fragrance-free to avoid additional sensitization.

treatments.name: Wet wrap therapy
treatments.result: For severe cases: Apply steroid, then damp cotton gloves, then dry gloves overnight
treatments.reasoning: Enhances steroid penetration and hydration. Useful for severe or refractory cases.

treatments.name: Oral antihistamine for itch (sedating at night)
treatments.result: Hydroxyzine 25mg at bedtime PRN for severe itch
treatments.reasoning: May help with sleep if itch is severe. Antihistamines don't treat the underlying dermatitis (Type IV hypersensitivity isn't histamine-mediated) but can help symptomatically.

treatments.name: Oral corticosteroid (for severe/widespread cases)
treatments.result: Prednisone taper if severe - 40mg x 5 days, 20mg x 5 days, 10mg x 5 days
treatments.reasoning: Reserve for severe, widespread, or vesicular contact dermatitis. Short taper to avoid rebound. Not for routine cases.

treatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus)
treatments.result: Consider for maintenance or steroid-sparing on thin skin areas
treatments.reasoning: Steroid-sparing option. No atrophy risk. Useful for face or long-term management. Can sting on application to inflamed skin.

consults.name: Dermatology referral
consults.result: Referral placed for patch testing and management of occupational contact dermatitis
consults.reasoning: Specialist referral indicated for: occupational cases (need documentation), refractory cases, need for patch testing, unclear diagnosis.

consults.name: Occupational Medicine
consults.result: May be needed for workplace accommodation documentation and workers' compensation evaluation
consults.reasoning: Occupational dermatitis causing disability requires formal documentation. May need workplace assessment for accommodation (glove policy, product changes).

consults.name: Allergist/Immunologist
consults.result: Alternative to dermatology for patch testing in some centers
consults.reasoning: Some allergists perform patch testing. Ensure they test relevant occupational allergens.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with follow-up
disposition.result: Follow up in 2-4 weeks. If not improved, expedite dermatology referral for patch testing.
disposition.reasoning: Most contact dermatitis managed outpatient. Follow-up ensures response and allows escalation if needed.

actualDiagnosis.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
keyFindings: Temporal relationship to new salon products
No satellite lesions or systemic symptoms
teachingPoints: Contact dermatitis pattern follows exposure - geometric borders, localized distribution
Detailed occupational and product exposure history is ESSENTIAL for diagnosis
Allergen avoidance is primary treatment - topical steroids are adjunctive
Hairdressers have highest rates of occupational contact dermatitis due to wet work and chemical exposures
Always examine web spaces and ask about household contacts to rule out scabies
Environmental history identifies the cause; without removing it, treatment will fail

closureScript.diagnosis: You have allergic contact dermatitis - an allergic reaction in your skin caused by contact with something, almost certainly one of the new products at your salon. Your skin has become sensitized to something in those products.
closureScript.plan: First, I need you to avoid the suspected products completely. Wear gloves for ALL wet work, not just coloring. I'm prescribing a medium-strength steroid cream to use twice daily for 2 weeks. Use gentle fragrance-free hand lotion multiple times a day. If this doesn't improve or keeps coming back, I'll refer you to dermatology for patch testing to identify exactly what you're allergic to.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you develop fever, spreading redness, pus, or increasing pain - these would suggest infection. Also return if this doesn't improve in 2 weeks.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know gloves are inconvenient, but barrier protection is the only way to prevent this from happening again. This may be a workers' compensation issue since it's caused by your work - let me know if you need documentation.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

male-45-radiographer.name: Vanessa Santos
male-45-radiographer.chiefComplaint: My hand is red and blistering and it's getting worse
male-45-radiographer.variantDescription: Industrial radiographer with radiation burn from source malfunction

missedConsequences.Radiation Burn: Continued progression to necrosis, may require amputation. Delayed recognition common.
missedConsequences.Necrotizing Fasciitis: Missed nec fasc is rapidly fatal.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about occupation and radiation exposure
Attributing to thermal or chemical burn without appropriate history
Not recognizing delayed onset as characteristic of radiation injury
Missing the sharp demarcation of radiation burns

allDiagnoses.name: Radiation Burn (Ionizing)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Industrial radiographer + source malfunction + delayed progressive burn + sharp demarcation = radiation burn.

allDiagnoses.name: Chemical Burn
allDiagnoses.rationale: No chemical exposure, delayed progression fits radiation.

allDiagnoses.name: Necrotizing Fasciitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Progressive necrosis requires consideration but too slow and no systemic symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Contact Dermatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Too severe with necrosis.

male-45-radiographer.oldcartsNarrative: "My hand started looking red about 3 weeks ago - I thought it was just a mild irritation. But it's gotten progressively worse. Now it's blistering and some areas look like the skin is dying - turning dark. I don't remember any burn or chemical exposure. I'm an industrial radiographer - I X-ray pipes and welds at factories. A few weeks ago the source didn't retract properly and I had to manually fix it."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first notice changes?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 weeks ago. Started as just some redness.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Delayed onset is characteristic of radiation burns

historyQuestions.text: How has it changed?
historyQuestions.answer: Got redder, then blistered, now some areas are dark and the skin is dying. It keeps getting worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive worsening even after exposure stops

historyQuestions.text: What exactly do you do at work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm an industrial radiographer. I do X-ray inspection of welds and pipes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High-risk occupation for radiation exposure

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your equipment
historyQuestions.answer: I use a portable X-ray source to inspect welds. Iridium-192. The source is in a container I hold.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms high-energy radiation source

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned a source malfunction?
historyQuestions.answer: A few weeks ago the source didn't retract properly into the shield. I had to manually secure it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: This is the exposure event - unshielded source

historyQuestions.text: What did your dosimeter show?
historyQuestions.answer: We have dosimeters but mine has been acting up. I sent it in for repair last month.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Malfunctioning dosimeter - no warning of exposure

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_current_job_title.answer: Industrial radiographer - I X-ray welds and pipes to check for defects.
occupation_current_job_title.relevance: Industrial radiographers work with high-energy radiation sources. Risk for radiation injury if safety protocols fail.

occupation_current_workplace_exposures.answer: I work with iridium-192 sources for radiographic testing.
occupation_current_workplace_exposures.relevance: Ir-192 is a high-energy gamma radiation source. Unshielded exposure causes severe radiation burns.

occupation_current_workplace_incidents.answer: A few weeks ago the source didn't retract properly into the shield. I had to manually secure it.
occupation_current_workplace_incidents.relevance: Exposed radiation source + manual handling = unshielded radiation exposure to hand.

occupation_current_ppe_use.answer: We have dosimeters but mine was malfunctioning.
occupation_current_ppe_use.relevance: Malfunctioning dosimeter meant no warning of radiation exposure.

physicalExam.finding: Anxious man guarding his right hand
physicalExam.reasoning: Concerned about injury

physicalExam.finding: Right hand: Sharply demarcated erythema on palm and fingers. Bullae and areas of epidermal sloughing. Central areas of purple/black discoloration (necrosis). Pattern consistent with grip location.
physicalExam.reasoning: Sharp demarcation + delayed progression + necrosis = radiation burn

physicalExam.finding: Radial pulse palpable but capillary refill prolonged in affected digits
physicalExam.reasoning: Vascular damage from radiation

labs.name: CBC with differential (serial)
labs.result: WBC 3.8 (low-normal), lymphocytes 800/μL (low - suggests significant exposure). Repeat daily.
labs.reasoning: Lymphocyte count is early indicator of radiation dose. Lymphopenia <1000 suggests significant exposure. Follow serially - nadir at 48-72h.

labs.name: Chromosomal analysis (biodosimetry)
labs.result: Dicentric chromosome analysis sent - results in 2-3 days. Will estimate whole-body dose.
labs.reasoning: Gold standard for dose estimation. Dicentric chromosomes are specific for ionizing radiation. Helps guide treatment and prognosis.

labs.name: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel
labs.reasoning: Baseline before supportive care. Monitor for tumor lysis-like syndrome in high-dose exposures.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT, Fibrinogen
labs.reasoning: Baseline - high-dose radiation can cause coagulopathy. Monitor if dose was significant.

labs.name: HLA typing
labs.result: Sent in case bone marrow transplant needed.
labs.reasoning: For potential stem cell transplant if whole-body dose was significant. Unlikely needed for localized hand exposure.

imaging.name: Hand X-ray
imaging.result: Soft tissue swelling. No bony abnormalities yet.
imaging.reasoning: Baseline imaging. Bone changes may appear later (weeks to months) with severe radiation injury.

imaging.name: CT angiography hand (if vascular compromise)
imaging.result: May be needed to assess vascular damage and plan reconstruction.
imaging.reasoning: Consider if digital ischemia worsens. Radiation damages microvasculature.

treatments.name: Aggressive wound care
treatments.result: Wound care with non-adherent dressings. Keep wounds clean and moist. Debride only necrotic tissue - conservative approach initially.
treatments.reasoning: Radiation wounds heal poorly. Conservative debridement - radiation injury may extend deeper than visible. Avoid aggressive surgery early.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: Radiation burns are extremely painful. May require opioids. Consider gabapentin for neuropathic component.
treatments.reasoning: Radiation burns cause severe pain from nerve damage and inflammation. Multimodal analgesia needed.

treatments.name: Pentoxifylline + Vitamin E (emerging therapy)
treatments.result: Consider pentoxifylline 400mg TID + vitamin E 1000IU daily for radiation fibrosis/injury.
treatments.reasoning: Some evidence for pentoxifylline + vitamin E combination in radiation injury. May improve microvascular flow and reduce fibrosis.

treatments.name: G-CSF (if significant systemic exposure)
treatments.result: Not indicated - this is localized exposure without significant bone marrow suppression.
treatments.reasoning: G-CSF for radiation-induced neutropenia from whole-body exposure. This is localized injury.

treatments.name: Antibiotics (if infection)
treatments.result: Watch for secondary infection. Start antibiotics if signs of cellulitis.
treatments.reasoning: Radiation wounds are immunocompromised and prone to infection. Low threshold to treat infection.

treatments.name: Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy
treatments.result: Consider HBO for radiation tissue injury - may improve wound healing.
treatments.reasoning: HBO can promote healing in radiation-damaged tissue. Often used for delayed radiation injuries and non-healing wounds.

treatments.name: Surgical intervention (debridement/amputation)
treatments.result: Burn surgery following. May need staged debridement or amputation if necrosis progresses.
treatments.reasoning: High-dose radiation burns often progress to necrosis requiring amputation. Wait for demarcation before definitive surgery. May need skin grafting or flaps.

consults.name: Radiation safety/Health physics
consults.result: Health physicist estimates dose to hand was 15-25 Gy based on Ir-192 source characteristics and estimated exposure time of 30-60 seconds.
consults.reasoning: Dose estimation crucial for prognosis. >25 Gy to hand likely requires amputation. Health physics can reconstruct exposure scenario.

consults.name: Burn/Hand Surgery
consults.result: Evaluating for staged debridement. Will follow closely for demarcation of necrosis. Amputation may be needed.
consults.reasoning: Specialized wound care needed. Radiation burns behave differently than thermal burns - progressive and slow to demarcate.

consults.name: REAC/TS (Radiation Emergency Assistance Center)
consults.result: Consulted REAC/TS at Oak Ridge for expert guidance on management.
consults.reasoning: REAC/TS provides 24/7 consultation for radiation injuries. Call 1-865-576-1005. Expert guidance invaluable.

consults.name: NRC/State radiation program notification
consults.result: Nuclear Regulatory Commission and state radiation control notified. Investigation initiated into equipment failure.
consults.reasoning: Mandatory reporting of radiation incidents. NRC will investigate equipment malfunction and employer safety practices.

consults.name: OSHA notification
consults.reasoning: Occupational injury requiring reporting. Investigation of safety violations.

disposition.name: Admission for wound care and monitoring
disposition.result: Admitted to burn unit/specialized wound care. Daily CBC monitoring. Burn surgery following.
disposition.reasoning: Significant radiation burn requires inpatient management. Progressive injury needs close monitoring.

disposition.name: Serial CBC monitoring
disposition.result: Daily CBC for 5-7 days to monitor lymphocyte count and detect systemic radiation effects.
disposition.reasoning: Even with localized injury, some systemic exposure may have occurred. Lymphocyte nadir helps estimate dose.

disposition.name: Psychological support
disposition.result: Social work and psychology consult. Radiation injury causes significant anxiety.
disposition.reasoning: Radiation exposure causes extreme anxiety. Possible loss of hand is devastating. Psychological support essential.

disposition.name: Workers' compensation
disposition.result: Social work to assist with workers' compensation claim. Occupational injury.
disposition.reasoning: Workplace injury requiring workers' comp. May involve prolonged disability.

disposition.name: Prognosis counseling
disposition.result: Counseled that radiation burns progress over weeks. May require multiple surgeries. Amputation possible if tissue continues to necrose. Long recovery expected.
disposition.reasoning: Set realistic expectations. 15-25 Gy to hand has poor prognosis - significant tissue loss likely. Ongoing monitoring for months.

disposition.name: Long-term follow-up
disposition.result: Will need long-term follow-up for: wound healing, late effects (fibrosis, contractures), and cancer surveillance.
disposition.reasoning: Radiation injuries can have late effects including fibrosis, contractures, and increased cancer risk. Lifelong surveillance needed.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Radiation Syndrome - Local (Radiation Burn)
keyFindings: Industrial radiographer working with Ir-192 source
Source failed to retract, required manual handling
teachingPoints: Radiation burns have DELAYED onset (hours to weeks after exposure)
Progression is relentless even after exposure stops
Sharp demarcation is characteristic (unlike thermal burns)
High-dose exposure causes vascular damage leading to progressive necrosis
Industrial radiographers at risk - Ir-192 and Co-60 are dangerous
Equipment failures + human error = radiation accidents
Treatment is supportive; severe cases need surgery/amputation
MUST report to radiation safety authorities immediately

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a radiation burn on your hand from that incident with the X-ray source a few weeks ago. When the source didn't retract and you had to handle it manually, you received a significant dose of radiation to your hand.
closureScript.plan: This type of injury gets worse before it gets better - that's the nature of radiation damage. We need to do careful wound care and I'm getting our hand surgeons involved. Depending on how the tissue responds, you may need surgery.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: I need to be honest with you - this is serious. Radiation burns can progress to the point where tissue dies and can't be saved. We're hoping to preserve as much function as possible, but I can't promise that.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: We've notified the radiation safety authorities - they need to investigate what went wrong with your equipment. Your employer should be ensuring this doesn't happen to anyone else. You may want to speak with a lawyer about workers' compensation.

guidelineReferences.name: REAC/TS Guidance for Radiation Accidents
guidelineReferences.citation: Radiation Emergency Assistance Center/Training Site (Oak Ridge)
keyPoints: Lymphocyte count at 48h estimates systemic dose
Local injuries may progress for weeks
Surgical debridement often delayed until demarcation

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

male-35-plating.name: Michael Rodriguez
male-35-plating.chiefComplaint: Painless nasal perforation and skin ulcers

missedConsequences.Chromium Toxicity: Continued exposure leads to lung cancer. Hexavalent chromium is carcinogenic.

allDiagnoses.name: Chromium Toxicity
allDiagnoses.rationale: Chrome plating + characteristic lesions.

allDiagnoses.name: Cocaine-induced Septal Perforation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must rule out - similar appearance.

allDiagnoses.name: GPA (Wegener's)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Autoimmune cause of septal perforation.

male-35-plating.oldcartsNarrative: "I've developed a hole in my nose - noticed when I blow my nose. Also have ulcers on my hands that won't heal. Work in chrome plating for 8 years - electroplating car parts. 'Chrome holes' is what my coworkers call these ulcers. We don't always wear gloves."

historyQuestions.text: What exactly do you do?
historyQuestions.answer: Chrome plating - dipping car parts in chromic acid baths. 8 years. Inadequate ventilation and PPE.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hexavalent chromium exposure.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_chemical_exposure.answer: Chrome plating with chromic acid for 8 years, poor PPE
occupation_chemical_exposure.relevance: Hexavalent chromium is corrosive and carcinogenic.

physicalExam.finding: 1cm septal perforation, clean edges, no active bleeding or crusting.
physicalExam.reasoning: Characteristic painless perforation from chromate inhalation.

physicalExam.finding: Multiple painless 'punched out' ulcers on knuckles and between fingers - 'chrome holes.'
physicalExam.reasoning: Pathognomonic skin lesions from direct contact.

imaging.text: What medications are you currently taking?
imaging.answer: Blood pressure medicine, I think lisinopril.
imaging.reasoning: Medications can cause or contribute to symptoms and affect treatment choices.

imaging.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
imaging.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing and treatment planning.

imaging.text: Do you have any chronic medical conditions?
imaging.answer: High blood pressure, that's about it.
imaging.reasoning: Past medical history affects differential diagnosis and management.

imaging.text: Have you had any prior surgeries?
imaging.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect treatment options.

treatments.name: Remove from exposure
treatments.result: Must change job or improve controls
treatments.reasoning: Stop ongoing damage.

treatments.name: Ascorbic acid topical
treatments.result: For skin ulcers - reduces Cr(VI) to Cr(III)
treatments.reasoning: Promotes healing.

consults.name: Occupational Medicine
consults.result: Workplace evaluation, lung cancer surveillance protocol

consults.text: What medications are you currently taking?
consults.answer: Blood pressure medicine, I think lisinopril.
consults.reasoning: Medications can cause or contribute to symptoms and affect treatment choices.

consults.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
consults.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing and treatment planning.

consults.text: Do you have any chronic medical conditions?
consults.answer: High blood pressure, that's about it.
consults.reasoning: Past medical history affects differential diagnosis and management.

consults.text: Have you had any prior surgeries?
consults.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect treatment options.

teachingPoints: Hexavalent chromium is CARCINOGENIC - lung cancer surveillance needed
Painless ulcers and septal perforation are characteristic
Must remove from exposure

contact_dermatitis.name: Contact Dermatitis

doorNote.chiefComplaint: Itchy rash on my hands for 1 week
doorNote.nurseNotes: Patient reports itchy, blistering rash on both hands. Works as a hairdresser. Started using new products at work 2 weeks ago. No fever or other symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: When did the rash start and how has it changed?
historyQuestions.answer: It started about a week ago with some redness and itching. Then little blisters appeared a few days later. It's been getting worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive course with vesicles suggests allergic contact dermatitis

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly is the rash?
historyQuestions.answer: It's on both hands - mostly on the back of my hands and between my fingers. A little bit on my wrists too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Distribution on dorsal hands and interdigital spaces suggests contact exposure

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your work - what do you do with your hands?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a hairdresser. I'm washing, cutting, and coloring hair all day. I wear gloves for coloring but not for shampooing.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hairdressers have high rates of occupational hand dermatitis

historyQuestions.text: Have you been exposed to anything new recently? New products?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! The salon started using a new brand of shampoo and conditioner about 2 weeks ago. That's right before this started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Temporal correlation with new product strongly suggests causation

historyQuestions.text: Do you wear gloves at work? What kind?
historyQuestions.answer: I wear latex gloves for color treatments. Maybe 10-15 times a day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Latex glove use raises possibility of latex allergy

historyQuestions.text: How itchy is it? Does anything make it better or worse?
historyQuestions.answer: It's REALLY itchy, especially at night. Water makes it sting. Scratching makes it feel better temporarily but then it gets worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Intense pruritus characteristic of allergic contact dermatitis

historyQuestions.text: Do you have the rash anywhere else on your body?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just my hands and a little on my wrists.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Localized distribution supports contact rather than systemic cause

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had eczema or sensitive skin?
historyQuestions.answer: I had some eczema as a kid but it went away. My skin has always been a little sensitive to certain soaps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: History of atopy increases susceptibility to contact dermatitis

historyQuestions.text: Have you tried anything for it?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been using over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream and it helps a little but not much.
historyQuestions.reasoning: OTC steroids often insufficient for allergic contact dermatitis

historyQuestions.text: Any fever, feeling unwell, or joint pains?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I feel fine otherwise. Just these itchy hands driving me crazy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out systemic causes of rash

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Normal vital signs. Afebrile.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal vitals expected for localized skin condition

physicalExam.finding: Well-appearing woman, frequently scratching her hands.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms pruritus severity

physicalExam.finding: Bilateral dorsal hand involvement with erythematous, edematous patches. Multiple small vesicles with some ruptured and weeping. Interdigital web spaces involved. Lichenification from scratching. Sharp demarcation at wrist (glove line).
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic appearance with vesicles and geometric distribution suggesting contact pattern

physicalExam.finding: Erythema and mild scaling at wrists, sharply demarcated. Pattern consistent with glove cuff line.
physicalExam.reasoning: Geometric pattern at glove line supports contact etiology

physicalExam.finding: Nails intact, no pitting or dystrophy.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal nails argue against psoriasis

physicalExam.finding: No rash elsewhere. No involvement of face, trunk, or feet. Some mild dryness on forearms.
physicalExam.reasoning: Localized distribution confirms contact rather than systemic

differentials.name: Dyshidrotic Eczema
keyFeatures: Would expect more palm/lateral finger involvement

contact_dermatitis.teachingPoint: Contact dermatitis approach: 1) Allergic vs irritant (allergic = vesicles, intense itch, delayed reaction; irritant = dryness, burning, immediate), 2) Identify the contactant (temporal correlation, geometric distribution, occupation), 3) AVOID the trigger (this is the most important treatment), 4) Treat inflammation (potent topical steroids, sometimes oral), 5) Consider patch testing if recurrent or unclear. For this hairdresser: new product is likely culprit, but consider latex allergy testing given frequent glove use.
contact_dermatitis.clinicalPearl: Look for the 'geometric distribution' - shapes that don't follow anatomical lines suggest external contact. The 'glove line' cutoff at the wrist is a giveaway. Hairdressers, healthcare workers, and food service workers have high rates of occupational hand dermatitis.

shingles.name: Herpes Zoster (Shingles)

doorNote.nurseNotes: Patient reports burning pain on left side for 4 days, then blistering rash appeared yesterday. Pain is severe. History of prostate cancer on surveillance. Taking blood pressure medication.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about when this started.
historyQuestions.answer: About 4-5 days ago I started getting this burning, tingling pain on my left side. I thought maybe I pulled a muscle. Then yesterday the rash appeared.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prodromal pain BEFORE rash is classic for zoster

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain.
historyQuestions.answer: It's like burning, stabbing pain. Even my shirt touching the skin hurts. It's the worst pain I've ever had.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Neuropathic pain (burning, allodynia) characteristic of zoster

historyQuestions.text: What does the rash look like?
historyQuestions.answer: It's a bunch of little blisters in a patch on my left side, kind of going from my back around to my belly. They're filled with clear fluid.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Grouped vesicles in dermatomal distribution = zoster

historyQuestions.text: Does the rash cross over your midline to the right side at all?
historyQuestions.answer: No, it stops right at the middle of my belly. Only on the left.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Respecting midline is pathognomonic for dermatomal process

historyQuestions.text: Did you have chickenpox as a child?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I had it when I was about 8 years old. Pretty bad case.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior varicella infection required for zoster reactivation

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned prostate cancer - are you on any treatment for that?
historyQuestions.answer: No, they're just watching it. My PSA has been stable. I'm not on chemo or anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Immunocompromised status would change management and prognosis

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with your eyes - vision changes, pain, or rash near your eye?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my eyes are fine. The rash is only on my side.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL to rule out V1 (ophthalmic) involvement

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with urination?
historyQuestions.answer: No, urination is normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sacral zoster can cause urinary retention

historyQuestions.text: Any weakness in your arms or legs?
historyQuestions.answer: No weakness.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Motor involvement is rare but serious complication

historyQuestions.text: Have you been around any pregnant women or people with weak immune systems?
historyQuestions.answer: My daughter is pregnant - she was visiting last week. Should I be worried?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Can transmit varicella to susceptible individuals - important for counseling

physicalExam.finding: Low-grade temp 99.4°F, otherwise normal. BP slightly elevated (pain-related).
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever can occur with zoster

physicalExam.finding: Elderly male appearing uncomfortable, guarding left side.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pain severity evident from behavior

physicalExam.text: Detailed skin examination of affected area
physicalExam.finding: Left T10 dermatome: Band of grouped vesicles on erythematous base, extending from posterior midline around flank to anterior midline. Vesicles vary from clear fluid to cloudy/pustular. Some crusting of older lesions. Does NOT cross midline. Surrounding skin with hyperesthesia - patient winces with light touch.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLASSIC dermatomal vesicular eruption with allodynia

physicalExam.finding: No lesions outside the T10 dermatome. No lesions on face, scalp, or elsewhere.
physicalExam.reasoning: More than 20 lesions outside primary dermatome = disseminated

physicalExam.finding: No vesicles on face, nose, or periorbital area. No Hutchinson's sign. Conjunctivae clear. Vision intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out V1 involvement - lesion on nose tip (Hutchinson sign) predicts eye involvement

physicalExam.finding: Mild left axillary lymphadenopathy.
physicalExam.reasoning: Regional lymphadenopathy common with zoster

differentials.name: Herpes Simplex
keyFeatures: Usually smaller, more localized, recurrent in same spot

differentials.name: Contact Dermatitis
keyFeatures: Wouldn't respect dermatome, wouldn't have prodromal pain

shingles.teachingPoint: Zoster management: 1) Antivirals within 72 hours of rash onset (valacyclovir 1g TID or acyclovir 800mg 5x/day for 7 days) - this patient is borderline but should still treat given severity, 2) Pain management (often need more than acetaminophen - gabapentin, TCAs, even opioids), 3) Counseling about contagiousness (cover lesions, avoid pregnant women and immunocompromised until crusted), 4) Consider Shingrix vaccine after acute episode resolves. Post-herpetic neuralgia risk increases with age - this patient at risk.
shingles.clinicalPearl: Three things to always check in suspected zoster: 1) Is it crossing the midline (if yes, consider disseminated or other diagnosis), 2) Is the eye involved (V1 dermatome - emergent ophthalmology referral), 3) Is the patient immunocompromised (changes management and prognosis). The pregnant daughter should avoid direct contact with lesions - she could develop primary varicella if not immune.

female-32-itchy.name: Amanda Clark
female-32-itchy.chiefComplaint: Itchy rash on my arms for 1 week
female-32-itchy.variantDescription: Contact dermatitis - approach to rash evaluation

missedConsequences.Contact Dermatitis: Usually benign but can become severe or infected if untreated.
missedConsequences.Drug Eruption: Can progress to Stevens-Johnson syndrome or DRESS if causative drug not identified.

commonPitfalls: Not describing the primary lesion morphology accurately
Missing the distribution pattern that gives away the diagnosis
Not asking about new exposures (contactants, medications)
Missing systemic symptoms that suggest drug reaction or infection

mcqs.question: Which distribution pattern is MOST consistent with contact dermatitis?

options.text: Scattered lesions on trunk following skin lines
options.explanation: This suggests pityriasis rosea ('Christmas tree' pattern).

options.text: Linear, geometric, or sharply demarcated pattern
options.explanation: Contact dermatitis follows the pattern of exposure - often linear (poison ivy) or matching the shape of the contactant (watch band, jewelry).
Suggests psoriasis or eczema.
Suggests tinea (ringworm).

mcqs.question: Which finding would make you MOST concerned for a systemic drug reaction (DRESS)?

options.explanation: Common in many rashes, not specific for drug reaction.

options.text: Fever, lymphadenopathy, and elevated liver enzymes
options.explanation: DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) presents with fever, lymphadenopathy, rash, and organ involvement (liver, kidney). Life-threatening.
Suggests contact dermatitis or herpes zoster.
Suggests inflammatory process responding to treatment.

allDiagnoses.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pruritic, geometric pattern, recent exposure history.

allDiagnoses.name: Irritant Contact Dermatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Similar presentation but non-immune mediated.

allDiagnoses.name: Drug Eruption
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must ask about new medications.

allDiagnoses.name: Cellulitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must distinguish from contact dermatitis - cellulitis is warm, tender, expanding.

allDiagnoses.name: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Chronic relapsing dermatitis, often history of atopy.

allDiagnoses.name: Psoriasis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale.

female-32-itchy.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this itchy rash on both forearms for about a week. It started as red bumps and now there are some blisters. It's so itchy I can't sleep. I noticed it a day or two after I was gardening - I was pulling weeds in shorts and short sleeves. I don't think I touched anything unusual but there might have been some vines. No new soaps or detergents. I haven't started any new medications. I tried hydrocortisone cream but it's not helping much."

historyQuestions.text: When did the rash start?
historyQuestions.answer: About a week ago. Maybe noticed it first 1-2 days after I was gardening.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Onset timing helps identify trigger. Allergic contact dermatitis typically appears 24-72 hours after exposure.

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly is the rash?
historyQuestions.answer: Both forearms, mainly. There are a few spots on my lower legs too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Distribution on exposed skin (forearms, legs) during gardening suggests plant contact dermatitis.

historyQuestions.text: Is it itchy, painful, or burning?
historyQuestions.answer: Extremely itchy. I've been scratching constantly. Not really painful.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pruritus is hallmark of contact dermatitis. Pain suggests infection.

historyQuestions.text: How has it changed since it started?
historyQuestions.answer: Started as red bumps, then got these little blisters. It seems to be spreading a little.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progression from papules to vesicles is typical of allergic contact dermatitis.

historyQuestions.text: Any new exposures - soaps, detergents, lotions, plants, metals?
historyQuestions.answer: I was gardening - pulling weeds, didn't wear gloves. There were some vines that could have been poison ivy but I tried to avoid them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLASSIC: Poison ivy/oak/sumac is most common cause of allergic contact dermatitis in US.

historyQuestions.text: Have you started any new medications in the last few weeks?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing new. Just my birth control which I've been on for years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Drug eruptions can occur even after prolonged use, but no new meds makes it less likely.

historyQuestions.text: Any fever, feeling unwell, joint pain, or mouth sores?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I feel fine otherwise. Just this itchy rash driving me crazy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Systemic symptoms would suggest drug reaction (DRESS, SJS) or systemic disease.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of eczema, allergies, or asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: I had eczema as a kid but not really as an adult. I have seasonal allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Atopic history increases susceptibility to contact dermatitis.

historyQuestions.text: Has this ever happened before?
historyQuestions.answer: I think I got poison ivy once as a teenager. This looks similar.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior poison ivy reaction means she's sensitized - subsequent exposures cause faster/stronger reactions.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Afebrile, vital signs normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Afebrile - argues against infection or systemic drug reaction.

physicalExam.finding: Primary lesions: Erythematous papules and vesicles, some coalescing into plaques. Vesicles contain clear fluid. Some areas with crusting (ruptured vesicles).
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic morphology: papules → vesicles → weeping/crusting.

physicalExam.finding: Distribution: Bilateral forearms, predominantly dorsal and lateral aspects. Linear streaks of vesicles. Scattered papulovesicles on anterior lower legs. Sharp cutoff at wrist (watch line) and mid-upper arm (sleeve line).
physicalExam.reasoning: LINEAR STREAKS = PATHOGNOMONIC for plant contact dermatitis (urushiol).

physicalExam.finding: Excoriations from scratching. No honey-colored crusting (no impetiginization). No warmth or induration beyond the rash.
physicalExam.reasoning: No signs of secondary bacterial infection.

physicalExam.finding: Mildly enlarged epitrochlear lymph nodes bilaterally. Non-tender. Mobile.
physicalExam.reasoning: Reactive lymphadenopathy can occur with contact dermatitis.

physicalExam.finding: No involvement of trunk, face, or genital area. No target lesions. No mucosal involvement.
physicalExam.reasoning: No target lesions or mucosal involvement - rules out Stevens-Johnson syndrome.

labs.name: No laboratory testing indicated for classic contact dermatitis
labs.result: Contact dermatitis is a clinical diagnosis based on history, distribution pattern, and morphology. Labs do not confirm or change management.
labs.reasoning: Classic poison ivy with linear vesicles on exposed skin after gardening requires NO laboratory workup. Diagnosis is clinical.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: Not indicated for typical contact dermatitis
labs.reasoning: Would only check if: concern for systemic drug reaction (DRESS - would show eosinophilia), suspected infection, or atypical presentation. Not needed here.

labs.name: Liver function tests
labs.result: Not indicated unless concern for systemic drug reaction
labs.reasoning: LFTs indicated if suspecting DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) - but this patient has clear contact exposure, no fever, no systemic symptoms.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.result: Imaging plays no role in contact dermatitis
imaging.reasoning: Contact dermatitis is diagnosed clinically. Imaging has no role.

procedures.name: No procedures required for classic presentation
procedures.result: Skin biopsy and patch testing not needed for obvious poison ivy dermatitis
procedures.reasoning: Classic linear vesicular eruption after plant exposure requires no biopsy or testing.

procedures.name: Patch testing
procedures.result: Consider referral to dermatology for patch testing if: recurrent contact dermatitis of unclear etiology, occupational dermatitis, or need to identify specific allergen for avoidance
procedures.reasoning: Poison ivy is obvious from history and appearance. Patch testing useful for: chronic/recurrent dermatitis, occupational exposures, suspected metal/fragrance allergy.

procedures.name: Skin biopsy
procedures.reasoning: Biopsy shows spongiotic dermatitis - nonspecific for eczema/contact dermatitis. Only useful if diagnosis uncertain or concern for other conditions (mycosis fungoides, vasculitis).

treatments.name: Decontamination (if within 30 minutes)
treatments.result: Too late for this patient (exposure >1 week ago). For fresh exposure: wash with soap and water within 30 minutes to remove urushiol. After 30 minutes, urushiol is bound to skin.
treatments.reasoning: Urushiol (the allergen in poison ivy/oak/sumac) binds to skin within 15-30 minutes. Washing is only effective immediately after exposure. Also wash clothes, tools, and pets that may have contacted plants.

treatments.name: High-potency topical corticosteroid
treatments.result: Clobetasol 0.05% ointment BID to affected areas for 2 weeks. Avoid face/groin (use lower potency there).
treatments.reasoning: For LOCALIZED contact dermatitis, high-potency topical steroids are first-line. Clobetasol, betamethasone dipropionate, or fluocinonide. Ointments better than creams for vesicular/weeping lesions.

treatments.name: Oral prednisone taper
treatments.result: Prednisone 1mg/kg (60mg for this patient) daily, taper over 2-3 WEEKS: 60mg x 5 days → 40mg x 5 days → 20mg x 5 days → 10mg x 3 days.
treatments.reasoning: For WIDESPREAD or SEVERE contact dermatitis (like this case with forearms and legs), oral steroids are indicated. CRITICAL: Taper over 2-3 weeks. Too-short course (5-7 days) causes REBOUND DERMATITIS.

treatments.name: Oral antihistamine for pruritus
treatments.result: Hydroxyzine 25mg at bedtime for itch. Daytime: non-sedating antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine).
treatments.reasoning: Antihistamines help with itch symptomatically. Contact dermatitis is T-cell mediated (not histamine), so antihistamines are adjunctive, not curative. Sedating antihistamines at night help with sleep.

treatments.name: Cool compresses and calamine lotion
treatments.result: Cool compresses with Burow's solution (aluminum acetate) or tap water for 15-20 min several times daily. Calamine lotion for soothing.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic relief. Cool compresses help dry weeping lesions. Avoid hot water (worsens itch).

treatments.name: Avoid OTC topical antihistamines and benzocaine
treatments.result: Counsel to AVOID topical Benadryl (diphenhydramine) and benzocaine products - can cause additional contact sensitization
treatments.reasoning: Topical diphenhydramine and benzocaine are common sensitizers and can cause additional allergic contact dermatitis on top of existing rash. Many patients try these OTC products - counsel to avoid.

treatments.name: Monitor for secondary infection
treatments.result: Watch for: increasing pain (vs itch), honey-colored crusting, fever, spreading warmth/redness. If present, add oral antibiotic coverage for staph/strep.
treatments.reasoning: Excoriation from scratching can lead to impetiginization (secondary bacterial infection). Signs: pain > itch, honey crusts, fever. Treat with cephalexin or dicloxacillin.

consults.name: No specialty consultation needed for typical contact dermatitis
consults.result: Primary care can manage straightforward allergic contact dermatitis
consults.reasoning: Classic poison ivy is managed in primary care. Dermatology referral not needed.

consults.name: Dermatology referral
consults.result: Consider for: refractory cases, recurrent dermatitis, occupational dermatitis requiring patch testing, atypical presentations
consults.reasoning: Refer for: not responding to treatment, unclear diagnosis, need for patch testing, concern for other conditions.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Discharge with topical and/or oral steroids. Follow up in 2 weeks or sooner if worsening/not improving. Return precautions for signs of infection.
disposition.reasoning: Contact dermatitis is outpatient diagnosis. Clear instructions on steroid taper (don't stop early), avoidance education, and return precautions.

actualDiagnosis.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis (Poison Ivy/Toxicodendron)
teachingPoints: Approach to rash: (1) Describe primary lesion, (2) Note distribution/pattern, (3) Ask about exposures and timing
Linear lesions = contact dermatitis until proven otherwise
Poison ivy, oak, sumac all contain urushiol - cross-react
Urushiol can persist on clothing/tools for months
Treatment: Wash off urushiol (ineffective after 30 min), topical steroids for mild, oral steroids for severe/widespread
Vesicle fluid does NOT spread the rash - it's not contagious
Oral prednisone taper must be long enough (2-3 weeks) or rash will rebound

revisedDifferential.name: Irritant Contact Dermatitis
keyFeatures: Would be at site of irritant, less vesicular

female-32-itchy.teachingPoint: This is classic poison ivy dermatitis. On your rotations, you'll see many rashes - the key is systematic description. 'Papulovesicular eruption in a linear distribution on sun-exposed extremities' - that phrase alone clinches the diagnosis. Treatment: mild/limited → topical steroids; moderate/widespread → oral prednisone 1mg/kg tapered over 2-3 weeks. Common mistake is too-short a steroid course causing rebound dermatitis.
female-32-itchy.clinicalPearl: The dermatology mantra: 'If it's linear, think external. If it's symmetric, think internal.' Linear lesions suggest something touched the skin (contact dermatitis, dermatomal zoster, arthropod bites in a line). Symmetric, bilateral eruptions suggest systemic cause (drug reaction, viral exanthem, autoimmune).

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.
